facts_disclosed: Considering moving to Islington to be closer to chambers

turns.content: I need to think through something practical. I've been looking at flats in Islington and I want to weigh up whether it actually makes sense.
Happy to help you think that through. What's driving the consideration — is it mainly about location and commute, or are there other factors like space, cost, or a change of scenery?

Commute, primarily. I work at chambers in Bedford Row and Islington would put me significantly closer. At the moment I'm in Hackney, which I love, but the journey in the mornings is starting to wear on me. I cycle when the weather cooperates, which is about twenty-five minutes, but otherwise it's the Overground and then the Tube — closer to forty-five with the changeover. From Islington I could walk to chambers in under half an hour.

Walking distance to work is genuinely transformative for quality of life, especially in a role with unpredictable hours. No worrying about last trains, no delays throwing off your morning. And the Angel to Holborn corridor is a pleasant walk.

What sort of properties have you been looking at, and is the cost difference manageable?

One-beds and small two-beds, mostly around Barnsbury and Canonbury. Rent is higher than what I pay now, but not catastrophically so — perhaps two to three hundred more a month. I could absorb it. The issue isn't really financial. It's Hackney. I've lived there for years. I know the neighbourhood properly — the market, the coffee shops, the park. I've got a life there that isn't connected to work. Moving to Islington feels like letting chambers consume another piece of my geography. I'm not sure I could leave Hackney without losing something I can't quite name.

That's a very real concern, and not one that shows up on a spreadsheet. There's a genuine psychological value in having your home life feel separate from your professional world. Hackney gives you that boundary — it's yours, not an extension of the Inns of Court.

Have you considered a middle path? Canonbury, for instance, has quite a different feel from the parts of Islington that border Holborn. It might give you some of the commute benefit without feeling like you've moved into chambers' postcode. And you don't have to frame it as permanent — a twelve-month tenancy lets you test it. You're not buying.

Canonbury is actually the area I'm most drawn to. It has a bit of a village quality. Trees, quieter streets. But then I walk past Broadway Market on a Saturday morning and think I'm mad for even considering leaving.

Broadway Market doesn't disappear because you've moved twenty minutes away. You could still go on Saturdays. It just shifts from being your doorstep to being a deliberate outing.

The reversibility matters here. If you try Canonbury for a year and miss Hackney too much, you move back. You haven't lost anything except a few months of slightly higher rent.

That's a fair point about the tenancy. I suppose I've been treating this as a permanent life decision when it needn't be. I've got two viewings booked next week — one on Canonbury Road and one off Alwyne Place. I'll go with an open mind rather than agonising about it in the abstract.

Good approach. When you view them, I'd suggest paying attention not just to the flat itself but to how the streets feel. Walk the route to Bedford Row from each one. Get a coffee nearby. See if you can picture a Saturday morning there. The practical logistics matter, but the gut feeling when you're standing in the neighbourhood is usually what tips the decision.

And for what it's worth — the fact that you're this attached to Hackney says something good about how you've built your life outside work. Whatever you decide, that instinct to protect your non-work identity is worth keeping.
Quite. I'll see how the viewings go. Thank you for helping me untangle it — I think I was just going round in circles on my own.
That's what the circles were for — sometimes you need to voice the competing pulls before you can weigh them properly. Good luck with the viewings next week. Trust the instinct.

One other thing before I go. Should I be looking at anything specific when I view a flat, beyond the obvious? I haven't moved in quite some time and I suspect I'll be distracted by whatever they've done with the staging.

Good question. A few things that are easy to overlook in the moment:

**Light** — visit at different times if you can, but at the very least notice which direction the main rooms face. North-facing living rooms can feel gloomy by November.

**Noise** — stand still for a minute and just listen. Road noise, neighbours, proximity to a pub. These things don't feature on a listing.

**Storage** — flats in that part of London can be generous with reception rooms and miserly with cupboard space. Think about where your life actually goes.

**Water pressure** — run a tap. It sounds pedestrian but it matters daily.

And ignore the staging entirely. You're assessing the bones of the place, not the cushions.
Water pressure. I would never have thought to check that. Rather glad I asked. I'll make a mental checklist and try not to be swayed by a well-placed bookshelf.

The well-placed bookshelf is exactly how they get you. Stay focused on the structure, the light, and the walk to Bedford Row. If both flats feel wrong, there's no obligation to pursue either. You're exploring the idea, not committing to it.
Exploring is the right word. I'm still not certain Islington is the answer, but at least now I have a sensible way to find out. I'll report back.
Do. And give yourself permission to come back and say it didn't feel right. There's no failure in deciding to stay where you're happy. The viewings are just information.
